title: RFC: Deepen panel target resolution
description:
## Problem

Panel target identity is a strong concept, but one module currently owns several concerns: target construction, equality, scene indexing, identity resolution, map projection, budget/timeline/consortium helper functions, and compatibility wrappers.

That makes the module useful but broad. Adding a target kind or changing map projection behavior risks touching unrelated target helper behavior. The wrapper module `panel-target-resolution.ts` is shallow because it mostly repackages the deeper module.

## Proposed Interface

Split panel target ownership into focused modules:

```ts
const index = panelTargetIdentity.indexScene(scene)
const resolution = panelTargetIdentity.resolve(targets, index)
const features = panelTargetMapProjection.project(resolution, location)
```

Keep target constructors and equality as the small identity interface. Move map feature projection into a map adapter. Move budget/timeline/consortium convenience helpers into domain adapters.

## Dependency Strategy

In-process. This is a local-substitutable seam: identity resolution can be tested without map projection, and map projection can be tested with a minimal resolved target input.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests:
- identity resolution handles species, placed plants, zones, manual, and none without side effects
- map projection projects only resolved scene refs and reports skipped/unresolved targets
- domain adapters produce the right target identity for budget, timeline, and consortium entries
- panel-origin hover/selection remains presentation-only and does not mutate canvas selection

Old tests to delete or collapse:
- wrapper tests that only assert `resolvePanelTargets()` forwards to `panelTargets.resolve()`.

Test environment needs:
- frontend Vitest tests for identity and projection modules
- existing panel target bridge authority tests retained as high-level guardrails

## Implementation Recommendations

Keep the canonical domain language: `PanelTarget` is identity, not selection. Resolution maps identity to current scene refs. Projection maps resolved refs to map features.

Do not reintroduce string matching against descriptions, legacy plant arrays, budget descriptions, or consortium canonical-name fields. Domain adapters should construct typed targets early and pass those through the resolver.
close_reason: Split panel target ownership into identity, map projection, and domain adapter modules; updated map overlay callers/docs and boundary tests. Gates: cd desktop/web && npm test -- panel-target-identity panel-target-resolution panel-target-map-projection panel-target-map-overlays panel-target-bridge-authority; cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen MapLibre surface lifecycle
description:
## Problem

The in-canvas MapLibre surface correctly has a single lifecycle owner and does not own document or canvas state. The lifecycle module is now accumulating several responsibilities: map creation, visibility gating, camera sync, basemap presentation, terrain sync, overlay sync, precision diagnostics, readiness state, async generation control, and error handling.

The module is deep today, but it is trending toward a coordinator hotspot. Future map work will be harder to test if each change must reason through the entire imperative lifecycle.

## Proposed Interface

Keep one lifecycle owner, but split internal adapters by responsibility.

Example shape:

```ts
surfaceLifecycle.update(snapshot)
cameraSync.apply(map, snapshot)
terrainSync.apply(map, snapshot.terrain)
overlaySync.apply(map, snapshot.targets)
surfaceState.publish(next)
```

The external lifecycle interface should remain small: `attach`, `update`, and `destroy`. Internally, map camera, basemap, terrain, overlays, and state publication should each have focused interfaces and tests.

## Dependency Strategy

In-process with internal seams. The existing `loadMapLibre` lazy adapter should remain the only MapLibre loading seam.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests:
- lifecycle creates/destroys the map only when visibility, location, runtime, and basemap style require it
- camera sync applies every viewport revision without deadbands
- terrain paint-only changes do not rebuild terrain sources
- overlay sync consumes pure panel-target map features and clears on disabled/error states
- generation cancellation prevents stale async terrain or map loads from mutating current state

Old tests to delete or collapse:
- broad lifecycle tests that inspect too many implementation details after behavior is covered by smaller adapter tests.

Test environment needs:
- Vitest tests with fake MapLibre map instances
- existing projection and panel-target map overlay tests

## Implementation Recommendations

Do not split ownership of the actual MapLibre instance. The lifecycle should remain the resource owner. The proposed split is internal: smaller modules receive a map and a snapshot and return/apply a result.

Preserve the current architectural rules:
- MapLibre is derived and non-authoritative
- camera math stays in the projection/camera seam
- lazy import boundary around `maplibre-gl` remains intact
- panel overlays consume resolved `PanelTarget` projection output, not direct scene identity logic
close_reason: Split MapLibre canvas overlay lifecycle work into a focused surface adapter while keeping lifecycle as the single map owner; added boundary tests for panel-target overlay sync and disabled/error clearing. Gates: cd desktop/web && npm test -- canvas-map-surface-overlays.test.ts canvas-map-surface-lifecycle.test.ts; cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen plant filter ownership
description:
## Problem

Dynamic plant filters are schema-driven through `common-types/plant-filter-fields.json`, generated TypeScript metadata, and a generated Rust SQL allowlist. Fixed filters still require parallel knowledge in `SpeciesFilter`, frontend derived state, fixed UI controls, and Rust SQL predicate code.

This leaves plant filtering with two overlapping interfaces: a deep generated dynamic filter interface and a shallow hand-maintained fixed filter interface. Adding or changing a filter can still drift across frontend counts, UI placement, IPC shape, SQL predicates, and option loading.

## Proposed Interface

Make the plant filter schema the single filter descriptor interface for both strip filters and dynamic filters.

Example shape:

```ts
const filterSet = plantFilterModel.fromUiState(state)
const visibleStripFields = plantFilterCatalog.fields({ placement: 'strip' })
```

Rust should consume the same generated field descriptors to build predicates, including field kind and operator support. Special domain filters like life cycle OR-semantics can remain explicit adapters, but they should be represented as named schema-backed filter strategies rather than freehand branches.

## Dependency Strategy

In-process. The existing generator remains the adapter between JSON schema, TypeScript metadata, and Rust SQL allowlist. No runtime dependency should be added.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests:
- every strip filter in the schema has frontend UI metadata and Rust predicate support
- every dynamic SQL field has a generated Rust allowlist entry and generated TS descriptor
- active filter count and clear-filter behavior are derived from the same filter model
- life cycle, climate zone, boolean, categorical, numeric, and range filters produce expected SQL plans

Old tests to delete or collapse:
- isolated active-filter-count tests that duplicate field lists, once a model-level test covers all configured fields.

Test environment needs:
- bindings generation check
- frontend plant browser tests
- Rust query-builder tests

## Implementation Recommendations

Do not remove the security property of `validated_column()`. The schema-driven module should deepen that protection by making unsupported fields impossible or visibly invalid at the model interface.

Migrate incrementally:
1. introduce a `plantFilterModel` that derives counts, emptiness, and request payloads from descriptors
2. move strip filters onto descriptor-driven state
3. replace fixed Rust predicate branches only where the descriptor can express the same semantics
4. leave explicit strategy adapters for genuinely domain-specific filters
close_reason: Implemented schema-backed plant filter ownership slice: added plantFilterModel/catalog for empty filters, active counts, request payload merging, and strip descriptor metadata; moved FilterStrip schema-backed rows through the catalog; tightened Rust query predicates to use generated field kind/column descriptors for schema-backed fixed filters and dynamic operator support. Gates passed: cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npx vitest run src/__tests__/plant-filter-model.test.ts src/__tests__/plant-filter-field-schema.test.ts src/__tests__/plant-search-session.test.ts src/__tests__/plant-db-controller.test.ts; cd desktop/web && npm test; cargo test query_builder; CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace; git diff --check. Generated contracts unchanged; gen:types/check:types skipped.

title: RFC: Deepen canvas runtime surfaces
description:
## Problem

The canvas runtime has good conceptual seams: command, query, and document surfaces. In practice, `currentCanvasSession` still publishes the full mounted runtime and the concrete `SceneCanvasRuntime` directly imports app-level signals, panel target state, and settings projection.

This weakens locality. Query-only consumers can be typed narrowly, but the runtime still carries orchestration concerns from the app shell. The deletion test says the surface types are earning their keep in tests, but the runtime implementation still knows too much about outer app state.

## Proposed Interface

Publish explicit runtime facade adapters instead of exposing the concrete mounted runtime everywhere.

Example shape:

```ts
const mounted = mountSceneCanvasRuntime(container, adapters)
setCanvasRuntimeSurfaces({
  commands: mounted.commands,
  queries: mounted.queries,
  documents: mounted.documents,
})
```

The concrete runtime should expose scene commands, scene queries, and document lifecycle methods. App-specific signal synchronization should sit in adapter modules around the runtime, not inside the runtime core.

## Dependency Strategy

In-process. This is a local-substitutable seam: tests can provide fake command/query/document adapters without constructing `SceneCanvasRuntime`.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests:
- command consumers cannot access query or document lifecycle methods
- query consumers cannot mutate document or canvas state
- document consumers cannot issue tool commands
- runtime facade adapters correctly bridge app signals without leaking the concrete runtime
- MapLibre and bottom-panel query consumers can run against a fake `CanvasQuerySurface`

Old tests to delete or collapse:
- tests that only prove `currentCanvasCommandSurface.value === runtime`; after the refactor, equality to the concrete runtime should no longer be the desired contract.

Test environment needs:
- focused Vitest tests for `canvas/session` publication
- existing canvas runtime tests for behavior preservation

## Implementation Recommendations

Keep `SceneStore` and `SceneCanvasRuntime` as the internal production implementation. Do not create a second scene owner.

Move app-signal synchronization behind explicit adapters:
- settings-backed toggles
- panel target hover/selection inputs
- canvas mirror signals
- runtime lifecycle publication

The external interface should make least-authority usage the default. Consumers should receive only `CanvasCommandSurface`, `CanvasQuerySurface`, or `CanvasDocumentSurface`, not `MountedCanvasRuntime`.
close_reason: Published explicit CanvasCommandSurface, CanvasQuerySurface, and CanvasDocumentSurface facade adapters instead of exposing the concrete mounted runtime through currentCanvasSession; document-session mounting now publishes facades and focused boundary tests assert consumers no longer receive SceneCanvasRuntime. Filed canopi-sfz for the remaining app-signal adapter extraction. Gates: cd desktop/web && npm test -- canvas-runtime-surfaces canvas-session canvas-session-selection use-canvas-document-session; cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen document file contract
description:
## Problem

The `.canopi` document interface is currently spread across Rust `CanopiFile`, TypeScript overrides, generated known keys, scene codec defaults, and document serialization merge logic. The authority split is correct, but adding a document field still requires maintainers to remember several separate update sites.

This makes the document module shallow: callers and future maintainers must know the implementation details of canvas-owned placeholders, document-owned sections, unknown-key preservation, and generated bindings to use the file format correctly.

## Proposed Interface

Create a deeper document file contract module that owns normalization, defaults, known-key discovery, and authority-aware composition.

Example shape:

```ts
const loaded = documentFileContract.normalizeLoaded(rawFile)
const saved = documentFileContract.composeForSave({
  metadata,
  canvas: sceneDocumentSnapshot,
  document: currentDocumentState,
})
```

The interface should hide:
- which fields are canvas-owned vs document-owned
- which required fields need canvas codec placeholders
- how `extra` preserves unknown fields
- how defaults are applied for backward-compatible required arrays
- how generated known keys stay aligned with the shared Rust contract

## Dependency Strategy

In-process. This should merge the current TS/Rust file-format helpers into a clearer module contract rather than adding a runtime dependency or external adapter.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests:
- loaded documents preserve unknown fields exactly once under `extra`
- save composition preserves document-owned timeline, budget, consortiums, location, description, and budget currency while taking canvas entities from the scene snapshot
- adding a representative required array/default field fails if known keys, defaults, and serialization are not aligned
- legacy fixtures still round-trip through the Rust migration path and frontend normalization

Old tests to delete or collapse:
- narrow tests that only assert scene codec placeholders if a new contract-level round-trip test covers the same behavior.

Test environment needs:
- frontend Vitest file-format contract tests
- Rust format migration tests when Rust-owned migration behavior changes

## Implementation Recommendations

The module should own file-format semantics, not canvas semantics. `SceneStore` should continue to own canvas state. The document file contract should accept canvas output as data, then compose it with document-owned state.

Move knowledge currently scattered across `desktop/web/src/types/design.ts`, `app/contracts/document.ts`, `canvas/runtime/scene/codec.ts`, `canvas/runtime/scene-runtime/document.ts`, and `bindings-gen` known keys into an explicit contract surface. Keep generated files generated, but make the generator consume one canonical field list or derive it from shared metadata where possible.
close_reason: Shipped frontend document file contract module owning known-key normalization, Rust-defaulted arrays, unknown-field deduplication, and authority-aware save composition; ran npm test -- document-format-contract.test.ts, npm test -- scene-runtime.test.ts canvas/runtime/scene-runtime.test.ts, npx tsc --noEmit, and npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen plant search query plan
description:
## Problem

The Rust plant search query module is partly deep, but query semantics still leak into `plant_db/search.rs`.

Current shallow modules and coupling:

- `query_builder::QueryBuilder` builds the list query and bound params.
- `build_count_query()` is separate and must stay behaviorally aligned with the list query filters.
- `plant_db/search.rs` independently determines relevance-offset pagination, computes total counts, executes the list query, maps rows, and derives the next cursor from sort-specific fields.
- Cursor derivation depends on `sort_column()` string values, which couples execution code back to SQL planning details.

The integration risk is query drift. Adding a filter, sort, or pagination mode requires touching multiple modules and keeping count/list/cursor behavior aligned by convention.

## Proposed Interface

Create a plant search query plan module that owns list SQL, optional count SQL, pagination mode, and next-cursor derivation.

Suggested shape:

```rust
pub struct SpeciesSearchRequest {
    pub text: Option<String>,
    pub filters: SpeciesFilter,
    pub cursor: Option<String>,
    pub sort: Sort,
    pub limit: u32,
    pub include_total: bool,
    pub locale: String,
}

pub struct SpeciesSearchPlan {
    pub list: SqlStatementPlan,
    pub count: Option<SqlStatementPlan>,
    pub page: SpeciesSearchPagePlan,
}

impl SpeciesSearchPlan {
    pub fn build(request: SpeciesSearchRequest) -> Self;
    pub fn next_cursor(&self, items: &[SpeciesListItem], has_next: bool) -> Option<String>;
}
```

Usage example:

```rust
let plan = SpeciesSearchPlan::build(request);
let total = execute_optional_count(conn, plan.count())?;
let rows = execute_species_list(conn, plan.list())?;
let next_cursor = plan.next_cursor(&items, has_next);
```

The module hides:

- FTS sanitization decisions
- whether relevance uses offset pagination
- count/list query parity
- cursor decoding and encoding
- sort-specific cursor value extraction
- limit-plus-one behavior

## Dependency Strategy

**In-process**: merge directly into the Rust DB query layer. No new crate or runtime dependency.

Execution can remain in `plant_db/search.rs`; planning should live under `db/query_builder/` so tests can exercise planning without an SQLite connection where possible.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests to write:

- Count and list plans use the same FTS/filter clauses.
- Relevance search uses offset pagination and derives the next offset cursor from returned item count.
- Non-relevance sorts use keyset pagination with sort-specific cursor values.
- Empty or unsafe FTS input falls back to non-FTS query planning.
- Existing SQL allowlist behavior for dynamic filters remains the query seam.

Old tests to delete or narrow:

- Tests that infer cursor behavior from `plant_db/search.rs` internals can move to query plan tests, leaving integration tests for execution and row mapping.

Test environment needs:

- Rust unit tests in `desktop/src/db/query_builder/` for planning.
- Existing in-memory SQLite tests in `plant_db/search.rs` for execution behavior.

## Implementation Recommendations

Keep SQL parameterization through `rusqlite::types::Value` and prepared statements. Do not weaken the generated dynamic filter allowlist.

Avoid broad SQL rewrites. First extract the current behavior into a deeper plan interface, then migrate `plant_db/search.rs` to execute the plan. Any query optimization should be a later bead unless required to preserve behavior.
close_reason: Implemented SpeciesSearchPlan query seam for list/count statements, relevance offset pagination, keyset cursor derivation, FTS fallback, and dynamic-filter allowlist coverage. Ran CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace and cargo test --workspace.

title: RFC: Deepen plant search session
description:
## Problem

The plant DB frontend search flow exposes async session mechanics across controller, state, and view modules.

Current shallow modules and coupling:

- `app/plant-browser/controller.ts` owns generation counters, debounce timers, in-flight state, pagination, result replacement, result appends, dynamic option caches, favorites, and sidebar lists.
- `app/plant-browser/state.ts` exports raw writable signals for search intent and committed data.
- `ResultsList.tsx` knows that `searchResultsRevision` means a committed first-page replacement and must drive virtualizer reset from that revision rather than raw intent state.

The integration risk is stale-data behavior. The app intentionally keeps old results visible while async searches are in flight, but that contract is enforced by convention across several callers instead of behind one interface.

## Proposed Interface

Create a plant search session module that separates intent state, in-flight request lifecycle, committed result state, and pagination append behind one interface.

Suggested shape:

```ts
export interface PlantSearchIntent {
  readonly text: string
  readonly filters: SpeciesFilter
  readonly extraFilters: readonly DynamicFilter[]
  readonly sort: Sort
  readonly locale: Locale
}

export interface PlantSearchResultState {
  readonly items: readonly SpeciesListItem[]
  readonly nextCursor: string | null
  readonly totalEstimate: number
  readonly committedRevision: number
  readonly status: 'idle' | 'loading-first-page' | 'loading-next-page' | 'error'
  readonly error: string | null
}

export interface PlantSearchSession {
  readonly intent: ReadonlySignal<PlantSearchIntent>
  readonly results: ReadonlySignal<PlantSearchResultState>
  setText(text: string): void
  patchFilters(patch: Partial<SpeciesFilter>): void
  setSort(sort: Sort): void
  retry(): void
  loadNextPage(): Promise<void>
  dispose(): void
}
```

Usage example:

```ts
const session = plantSearchSession.current
session.setText(nextText)
const results = session.results.value
virtualizer.resetWhen(results.committedRevision)
```

The module hides:

- debounce timing
- generation/cancellation policy
- first-page replacement versus pagination append
- committed-data revision semantics
- `include_total` rules
- stale visible data while a new request is in flight

## Dependency Strategy

**Local-substitutable**: inject the plant search adapter and timer functions for tests.

Production uses the existing IPC adapter (`searchSpecies`) and settings locale signal. Tests use a fake search adapter and fake timers to exercise stale response ordering and pagination.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests to write:

- Text changes debounce first-page searches.
- Filter/sort/locale changes trigger immediate or intended first-page replacement behavior.
- Stale first-page responses are ignored.
- Pagination appends items without incrementing committed first-page revision.
- Errors preserve visible stale items where intended and report status correctly.
- `ResultsList` only depends on committed revision and item count, not raw query signals.

Old tests to delete or narrow:

- Controller tests that assert internal generation counters or raw signal choreography can be replaced with session-state transition tests.

Test environment needs:

- Vitest fake timers and mock IPC adapter.
- Existing focused plant DB tests remain the quality gate.

## Implementation Recommendations

Keep favorites and recently viewed as a separate follow-up unless the initial extraction requires moving them to avoid confusing ownership. The first deepening pass should focus on search intent, committed result state, pagination, and dynamic filter option request state.

Do not regress the async surface rule: scroll reset and virtualizer reset must continue to key off committed first-page data, not raw intent changes.
close_reason: Implemented the plant search session seam with injected search and dynamic-option adapters, async generation/debounce ownership, committed result revision semantics, pagination append handling, session-driven plant DB views, focused seam tests, npx tsc --noEmit, focused Vitest, and full npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen panel target identity
description:
## Problem

PanelTarget is the shared identity language between timeline, budget, consortium, canvas highlights, and map overlays, but its behavior is spread across several shallow modules.

Current shallow modules and coupling:

- `panel-targets.ts` owns constructors, equality, and some domain-specific helpers.
- `app/panel-targets/coordinator.ts` owns hover/selection signal coordination.
- `panel-target-resolution.ts` maps targets to scene IDs.
- `panel-target-map-projection.ts` repeats target iteration to convert resolved targets into map features.
- `maplibre/panel-target-overlays.ts` builds a projection scene and overlay contract separately.

The integration risk is identity drift. Adding or changing a target kind requires updates across equality, migration, panel coordination, scene resolution, and map projection. The current seam has leverage, but the interface is still too fragmented for such a load-bearing domain concept.

## Proposed Interface

Create a deeper panel target identity module that owns target semantics and target-to-scene resolution. Keep MapLibre rendering as an adapter that consumes map-ready target features.

Suggested shape:

```ts
export interface PanelTargetSceneIndex {
  readonly plantsById: ReadonlyMap<string, PanelTargetPlantRef>
  readonly plantIdsBySpecies: ReadonlyMap<string, readonly string[]>
  readonly zonesByName: ReadonlyMap<string, PanelTargetZoneRef>
}

export interface PanelTargetResolution {
  readonly plantIds: readonly string[]
  readonly zoneIds: readonly string[]
  readonly sceneIds: readonly string[]
  readonly unresolvedTargets: readonly PanelTarget[]
  toMapFeatures(location: PanelTargetMapProjectionLocation | null): PanelTargetMapProjectionResult
}

export const panelTargets: {
  species(canonicalName: string): SpeciesPanelTarget
  key(target: PanelTarget): string
  equals(left: PanelTarget, right: PanelTarget): boolean
  listEquals(left: readonly PanelTarget[], right: readonly PanelTarget[]): boolean
  indexScene(scene: ScenePersistedState): PanelTargetSceneIndex
  resolve(targets: readonly PanelTarget[], index: PanelTargetSceneIndex): PanelTargetResolution
}
```

Usage example:

```ts
const index = panelTargets.indexScene(scene)
const resolved = panelTargets.resolve(selectedTargets, index)
renderer.highlight(resolved.sceneIds)
mapOverlay.sync(resolved.toMapFeatures(location))
```

The module hides:

- target key formatting
- equality semantics
- species-to-placed-plant expansion
- unresolved target handling
- empty semantics for `manual` and `none`
- deduplication across plant and zone IDs

## Dependency Strategy

**Local-substitutable**: pure target identity behavior should be testable with small in-memory scene indexes.

The state coordinator can remain a thin adapter around signals. MapLibre overlay sync should stay an adapter that consumes projection results rather than owning identity resolution.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests to write:

- Target constructors, keys, equality, and list equality are covered in one place.
- Scene indexing resolves placed plant, species, zone, manual, and none targets consistently.
- Map feature projection consumes the same resolution path as canvas highlights.
- Duplicate targets emit stable deduped scene IDs and map features.
- Missing plants/zones produce unresolved or skipped results without mutating hover/selection state.

Old tests to delete or narrow:

- Tests duplicated across panel-target resolver, map projection, and overlay contract can collapse around the shared identity interface.

Test environment needs:

- Pure frontend unit tests; no canvas runtime or MapLibre instance required.

## Implementation Recommendations

Do not make PanelTarget state authoritative for real canvas selection. The panel-origin hover/selection signals remain presentation state only. This refactor should concentrate identity semantics, not change selection ownership.

Keep `projectPanelTargetsToMapFeatures()` as a compatibility wrapper during migration, then retire it after all callers use the deeper identity module.
close_reason: Implemented the deep panelTargets identity seam with scene indexing, shared resolution, map-feature projection, compatibility adapters, focused seam tests, npx tsc --noEmit, focused Vitest, and full npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen canvas scene edit transactions
description:
## Problem

Canvas scene edits repeat the same mutation lifecycle in several modules: capture a snapshot, update `SceneStore`, update selection or locks, record history, increment `sceneEntityRevision`, and invalidate rendering.

Current shallow modules and coupling:

- `scene-runtime/mutations.ts` repeats capture/mutate/mark-dirty/invalidate across command-style edits.
- `scene-interaction.ts` owns drag, rectangle, drop, stamp, and text edit lifecycle details directly.
- `scene-runtime/document.ts` is the only place that increments `sceneEntityRevision` during dirty marking, but callers still have to remember to route through it.
- `scene-runtime.ts` wires these responsibilities together with callback bundles, making the interface almost as detailed as the implementation.

The integration risk is runtime consistency: a new canvas mutation path can update scene state but miss history, dirty state, selection sync, revision bumps, or the correct invalidation kind.

## Proposed Interface

Create a scene edit transaction module owned by the canvas runtime implementation.

Suggested shape:

```ts
export interface SceneEditTransaction {
  mutate(edit: (draft: ScenePersistedState) => void): void
  setSelection(ids: Iterable<string>): void
  setLockedIds(ids: Iterable<string>): void
  commit(options?: { type?: string; invalidate?: 'scene' | 'viewport' | 'chrome' }): boolean
  abort(): void
  readonly changed: boolean
}

export interface SceneEditCoordinator {
  run(type: string, edit: (tx: SceneEditTransaction) => void): boolean
  begin(type: string): SceneEditTransaction
}
```

Usage example:

```ts
sceneEdits.run('delete-selected', (tx) => {
  tx.mutate((draft) => removeSelectedEntities(draft, selectedIds))
  tx.setSelection([])
})

const drag = sceneEdits.begin('interaction-drag')
drag.mutate((draft) => applySceneDragDelta(draft, dragState, delta))
drag.commit({ invalidate: 'scene' })
```

The module hides:

- snapshot capture and restore
- no-op detection
- history command creation
- `sceneEntityRevision` increments
- selection and lock synchronization
- render invalidation after a committed edit

## Dependency Strategy

**In-process**: merge directly into the canvas runtime implementation. No new runtime dependency and no public app-facing surface growth.

The transaction coordinator can use existing `SceneStore`, `SceneHistory`, `SceneRuntimeDocumentBridge`, and render scheduler internals. It should remain private to `SceneCanvasRuntime` unless a concrete second adapter appears.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests to write:

- A committed edit records exactly one history entry and increments `sceneEntityRevision` once.
- A no-op edit does not dirty history or invalidate rendering.
- Abort restores persisted scene, session selection, and locks.
- Command edits and interaction edits share the same transaction behavior.
- Undo/redo still roundtrip through the existing history runtime.

Old tests to delete or narrow:

- Repetitive tests that spy on individual `_invalidate()` calls for each mutation path can shrink once transaction behavior is covered.

Test environment needs:

- Existing Vitest scene runtime tests with fake renderer host where possible.
- No Tauri backend required.

## Implementation Recommendations

Keep geometry, hit testing, and pointer policy outside the transaction module. The transaction module should own edit lifecycle only: before-state, after-state, history, revision, and invalidation.

Do this in small steps: introduce the coordinator, migrate one command path, then interaction paths, then remaining command paths. Preserve the existing `CanvasCommandSurface`, `CanvasQuerySurface`, and `CanvasDocumentSurface` split.
close_reason: Implemented the scene edit transaction coordinator, routed command and interaction scene edits through it, updated renderer ownership docs, and ran focused Vitest, npx tsc --noEmit, and full npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen non-canvas edit transactions
description:
## Problem

The timeline and consortium tools both perform direct-manipulation edits by mutating document arrays with `markDirty: false`, then relying on the mounted UI surface to call `markDocumentDirty()` on mouseup and unmount.

Current shallow modules and coupling:

- `app/timeline/controller.ts` and `app/consortium/controller.ts` expose low-level update helpers with a `markDirty` escape hatch.
- `InteractiveTimeline.tsx` and `ConsortiumChart.tsx` own preview-vs-commit policy, drag cleanup, and dirty-state finalization.
- `app/document/controller.ts` exposes revision mutation directly, so callers must know when a preview mutation should or should not advance the non-canvas dirty baseline.

This spreads document edit lifecycle knowledge across the panel UI and document controller seam. The integration risk is save/autosave correctness: a preview mutation can become durable without the dirty revision being advanced if one cleanup path is missed.

## Proposed Interface

Create a non-canvas edit transaction module that owns preview, commit, abort, and dirty marking for document-array edits.

Suggested shape:

```ts
export interface DocumentArrayEditTransaction<K extends keyof CanopiFile> {
  preview(updater: (items: CanopiFile[K]) => CanopiFile[K]): void
  commit(): void
  abort(): void
  readonly hasMutated: boolean
}

export function beginDocumentArrayEdit<K extends 'timeline' | 'consortiums'>(
  key: K,
): DocumentArrayEditTransaction<K>
```

Usage example:

```ts
const dragEdit = beginDocumentArrayEdit('timeline')
dragEdit.preview((timeline) => moveTimelineAction(timeline, actionId, nextDates))
// on mouseup
if (dragEdit.hasMutated) dragEdit.commit()
// on cancel/unmount
else dragEdit.abort()
```

The module hides:

- `markDirty: false` preview writes
- when `nonCanvasRevision` advances
- restoring the pre-drag document slice on abort
- idempotent commit/abort cleanup

## Dependency Strategy

**In-process**: merge directly into the frontend document layer. No new runtime dependency.

The transaction module can depend on `currentDesign`, `mutateCurrentDesign`, and `markDocumentDirty`, but callers should only depend on the transaction interface. Domain controllers can provide pure array updaters that are reused by transaction previews.

## Testing Strategy

New boundary tests to write:

- Preview updates visible document state without advancing `nonCanvasRevision`.
- Commit advances `nonCanvasRevision` exactly once after one or many preview mutations.
- Abort restores the original document slice and does not dirty the document.
- Unmount cleanup can call commit/abort idempotently.
- Timeline and consortium drag flows use the transaction module instead of manual dirty calls.

Old tests to delete or narrow:

- Tests that assert individual panel cleanup calls `markDocumentDirty()` can be replaced with transaction behavior tests.

Test environment needs:

- Pure signal-based frontend tests; no Tauri or canvas runtime required.

## Implementation Recommendations

The module should own the document edit lifecycle, not pointer geometry. Timeline and consortium modules should continue owning domain-specific array transformations, but the panel surfaces should stop coordinating dirty-state semantics directly.

Keep the first implementation scoped to `timeline` and `consortiums`. Budget edits are ordinary immediate document mutations today and should not be dragged into this refactor unless a direct-manipulation budget interaction appears.
close_reason: Implemented non-canvas document array edit transactions for timeline and consortium drags; added focused transaction tests; ran npx tsc --noEmit and npm test.

title: Epic: Deepen architecture seams
description:
## Problem

The current architecture is healthy enough to evolve, but several important seams are still shallow:

- Direct-manipulation document edits leak dirty-state and preview/commit rules into UI modules.
- Canvas scene edits repeat transaction mechanics across interaction and command paths.
- Panel target identity is spread across creation, equality, coordination, scene resolution, and map projection helpers.
- Plant search exposes async generation, committed-result revision, pagination, and virtualizer reset concerns across state/controller/view modules.
- Rust plant search query planning splits list SQL, count SQL, pagination mode, and cursor derivation across multiple modules.

These are not feature gaps. They are deepening opportunities: make the interface smaller and move behavior behind the seam so callers get more leverage and maintainers get better locality.

## Child RFCs

This epic tracks five child decision beads:

- Deepen non-canvas edit transactions
- Deepen canvas scene edit transactions
- Deepen panel target identity
- Deepen plant search session
- Deepen plant search query plan

## Implementation Strategy

Treat each child as independently grabbable. Do not bundle the implementation into one broad rewrite. Each child should preserve the existing product behavior while moving repeated policy behind a deeper module interface.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each child bead has a concrete RFC body with problem, proposed interface, dependency strategy, testing strategy, and implementation guidance.
- Each child can be implemented in a scoped branch with focused quality gates.
- The epic remains open until the child RFCs are accepted or explicitly rejected.
close_reason: All five architecture seams are accepted, implemented, and merged: non-canvas document edit transactions, canvas scene edit transaction coordinator, panelTargets identity/resolution/projection, plant search session lifecycle, and SpeciesSearchPlan query planning. Child gates recorded across the epic: focused frontend seam tests, npx tsc --noEmit, full npm test, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace, and cargo test --workspace. Epic closure is metadata-only.

title: RFC: Deepen Settings Projection
description:
## Problem

Settings persistence is shallow because callers and the persistence module share too many details about field mapping, normalization, and persistence timing.

Current friction:
- Hydration manually maps every Rust `Settings` field into frontend projection signals.
- Persistence manually reconstructs every field from those signals.
- Callers must remember whether a mutation should persist immediately, queue persistence, or skip persistence for hot paths.
- UI modules can mutate settings-backed signals directly and then call persistence as a second step.

The interface is close to the implementation: adding one setting requires edits across the Rust type, TS type, signal state, hydration, persistence snapshot, and every mutating caller.

## Proposed Interface

Create a settings projection module that owns the frontend projection of Rust settings:

```ts
type SettingsPersistMode = 'immediate' | 'queued' | 'none'

interface SettingsProjectionDraft {
  locale: Locale
  theme: Theme
  basemapStyle: BasemapStyle
  snapToGrid: boolean
  snapToGuides: boolean
  autoSaveIntervalMs: number
  bottomPanel: { open: boolean; height: number; tab: BottomPanelTab }
  mapLayers: {
    baseVisible: boolean
    baseOpacity: number
    contoursVisible: boolean
    contoursOpacity: number
    contourIntervalMeters: number
    hillshadeVisible: boolean
    hillshadeOpacity: number
  }
}

function hydrateSettingsProjection(settings: Settings): void
function snapshotSettingsProjection(): Settings
function mutateSettingsProjection(
  mutate: (draft: SettingsProjectionDraft) => void,
  options?: { persist?: SettingsPersistMode; delayMs?: number },
): void
function flushSettingsProjection(): void
```

Usage example:

```ts
mutateSettingsProjection((settings) => {
  settings.mapLayers.baseOpacity = opacity
}, { persist: 'queued' })
```

The module hides signal writes, normalization, Rust field naming, queued timers, and no-op persistence checks.

## Dependency Strategy

**In-process**: one projection module maps between Rust `Settings` and frontend signals.

Dependencies are handled through:
- Existing frontend signals as implementation details.
- `set_settings` IPC as the production persistence adapter.
- A local fake persistence adapter in tests.

The Rust user DB remains the source of truth; frontend signals remain runtime projections.

## Testing Strategy

- **New seam tests to write**: full hydration from Rust settings, full snapshot back to Rust settings, normalization of theme/map style/opacities/intervals, immediate persistence, queued persistence debounce, flush-on-teardown, no persistence before bootstrap, no-op mutation avoids IPC, direct setting actions call the projection module rather than writing signal plus persistence separately.
- **Old tests to delete**: per-caller tests that only verify `persistCurrentSettings()` was called after manual signal writes.
- **Test environment needs**: fake `set_settings` adapter and resettable signal fixtures.

## Implementation Recommendations

- The module should own all settings-backed signal writes.
- Prefer named mutation functions for common actions only where they add domain language; otherwise use the draft mutation seam.
- Make persistence timing explicit in the interface so callers do not need to know timer implementation details.
- Keep first-paint theme cache separate from authority; Rust settings still overwrite projection on bootstrap.
- Migrate settings-backed actions first, then remove direct signal writes from UI chrome.
close_reason: Implemented frontend settings projection seam: Rust Settings hydrate/snapshot mapping, normalized projection writes, immediate/queued/flush persistence, no-op IPC checks, first-paint theme cache priming, and migrated settings-backed callers through mutateSettingsProjection. Gates passed: cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npx vitest run src/__tests__/settings-projection.test.ts src/__tests__/canvas-actions.test.ts src/__tests__/layer-panel.test.tsx src/__tests__/commands-registry.test.ts src/__tests__/shell-close-guard.test.ts src/__tests__/use-canvas-document-session.test.tsx; cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen Plant Filter Field Schema
description:
## Problem

Plant filter field metadata is spread across shallow modules in two languages. The More Filters UI, Rust SQL allowlist, Rust field kind detection, strip exclusion rules, value ordering, translation keys, and schema contract all encode overlapping knowledge.

Current friction:
- Adding a dynamic filter requires editing multiple field lists and remembering which list controls security, UI category, field kind, translation, and ordering.
- Drift is possible: a field can appear in the TS registry but not the Rust allowlist, producing runtime backend mismatch errors.
- Boolean and numeric classification is duplicated separately from SQL allowlisting.
- Strip-only fields require caller knowledge to avoid duplicate filtering.

The interface is shallow because callers must understand implementation-specific field metadata instead of asking one plant filter schema module.

## Proposed Interface

Create one plant filter field schema contract with generated Rust and TypeScript adapters:

```json
{
  "fields": [
    {
      "key": "height_max_m",
      "kind": "numeric",
      "category": "climate",
      "i18n_key": "filters.field.heightMax",
      "ui_placement": "dynamic",
      "step": 0.5,
      "sql_column": "s.height_max_m",
      "ordering": null
    }
  ]
}
```

Generated TypeScript interface:

```ts
interface PlantFilterFieldDef {
  key: string
  kind: 'boolean' | 'categorical' | 'numeric'
  category: FilterCategory
  i18nKey: string
  uiPlacement: 'strip' | 'dynamic' | 'hidden'
  colorToken: string
  step?: number
  ordering?: string | null
}

function dynamicFilterFieldsForCategory(category: FilterCategory): PlantFilterFieldDef[]
function isStripField(key: string): boolean
```

Generated Rust interface:

```rust
pub struct PlantFilterField {
    pub key: &'static str,
    pub column: &'static str,
    pub kind: PlantFilterFieldKind,
}

pub(crate) fn filter_field(key: &str) -> Option<&'static PlantFilterField>;
pub(crate) fn validated_column(key: &str) -> Option<&'static str>;
pub(crate) fn filter_field_kind(key: &str) -> Option<PlantFilterFieldKind>;
```

The TS adapter omits SQL columns from shipped UI metadata. The Rust adapter keeps SQL column access as the security seam.

## Dependency Strategy

**Ports & adapters**: one contract generates two adapters.

Dependencies are handled by:
- A repo-owned filter-field contract file as the source module.
- A Rust adapter for SQL allowlisting and dynamic option kind detection.
- A TypeScript adapter for More Filters categories, labels, strip/dynamic placement, and ordering metadata.
- Existing i18n files remain the translation value adapter.

## Testing Strategy

- **New seam tests to write**: generated TS fields match generated Rust keys, every dynamic TS field has a Rust allowlisted column, every Rust dynamic field has a declared kind, strip fields do not appear in More Filters, every field has an i18n key in all supported locales, numeric/boolean/categorical options are produced from the schema, `validated_column()` rejects unknown fields.
- **Old tests to delete**: tests that only guard one list while allowing drift in the other lists.
- **Test environment needs**: codegen check similar to existing type binding checks, plus Rust unit tests for `filter_field()` and frontend unit tests for field grouping.

## Implementation Recommendations

- Do not weaken the SQL allowlist; generated Rust code should still expose only static vetted column strings.
- Keep junction-table filters like climate zones explicit as strip fields unless the schema grows a dedicated relation-filter shape.
- Keep More Filters rendering data-driven; it should consume the generated TS adapter, not hardcoded field arrays.
- Move value-ordering metadata into the field schema after the base field contract is generated.
- Migrate one field category at a time only if codegen is too large for a single safe patch.
close_reason: Implemented plant filter field schema contract with generated TypeScript UI metadata and generated Rust SQL allowlist/kind adapter. Migrated More Filters grouping, strip placement, field kind handling, option ordering, ActiveChips metadata, validated_column(), and dynamic option kind detection to the generated schema while keeping SQL columns out of shipped TS metadata. Gates passed: cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npx vitest run src/__tests__/plant-filter-field-schema.test.ts src/__tests__/more-filters-panel.test.tsx src/__tests__/active-chips.test.tsx; cd desktop/web && npm test; cd desktop/web && npm run gen:types; cd desktop/web && npm run check:types; CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace; cargo test --workspace; git diff --check.

title: RFC: Deepen Document Session Transition
description:
## Problem

Document session transition logic is shallow because callers still need to know the sequencing across document actions, runtime hydration, dirty baselines, queued loads, chrome visibility, history, and document workflows.

Current friction:
- Document replacement and mount-time hydration cross different modules but must preserve the same post-load behaviour.
- The document session module has two load paths that both know about `zoomToFit()` and workflow installation.
- Queued loads bypass the dirty guard, but still need the same runtime/document state handoff.
- Runtime mount currently monkey-patches `loadDocument()` and `replaceDocument()` to discover whether a real document was loaded before teardown.

This makes the document session harder to navigate because the interface is not deep enough: deleting the helper modules would spread the same sequence across callers, but the current sequence is still visible to too many callers.

## Proposed Interface

Create a document session transition module whose interface owns document replacement as one operation:

```ts
type DocumentTransitionSource = 'new' | 'open-path' | 'open-dialog' | 'template' | 'queued-path' | 'queued-template' | 'mount-existing'

type DirtyGuardMode = 'confirm' | 'skip'

interface DocumentTransitionRequest {
  source: DocumentTransitionSource
  dirtyGuard: DirtyGuardMode
  session: CanvasDocumentSurface
  load: () => Promise<{ file: CanopiFile; path: string | null; name: string }>
  isCancelled?: () => boolean
}

interface DocumentTransitionResult {
  status: 'applied' | 'cancelled' | 'queued' | 'failed'
  documentLoaded: boolean
}

async function transitionDocument(request: DocumentTransitionRequest): Promise<DocumentTransitionResult>
```

Usage example:

```ts
await transitionDocument({
  source: 'open-path',
  dirtyGuard: 'confirm',
  session,
  load: async () => {
    const file = normalizeLoadedDocument(await loadDesign(path))
    return { file, path, name: file.name }
  },
})
```

The module hides:
- dirty confirmation and save-before-replace semantics
- cancellation normalization
- document normalization
- runtime `loadDocument` vs `replaceDocument` choice
- current document signal replacement
- dirty baseline reset
- history reset
- chrome visibility
- post-load zoom
- workflow installation
- queued-load cleanup and error reporting
- whether the mounted runtime has ever loaded a real document

## Dependency Strategy

**In-process**: document transition is pure frontend orchestration around existing IPC and runtime modules.

Dependencies are handled through small local inputs:
- `CanvasDocumentSurface` for document lifecycle operations.
- A loader callback for new/open/template variants.
- A confirmation adapter for save/discard/cancel.
- A notification adapter for queued-load failures.

No mock seam is needed for Tauri IPC inside the transition module; tests pass loader callbacks directly.

## Testing Strategy

- **New seam tests to write**: dirty save/discard/cancel, open path, open dialog, template import, queued path, queued template, mount-existing hydration, cancellation after async load, failed queued load restoration, workflow installed before load completes, zoom applied exactly once per loaded document, history cleared on replacement, dirty baselines reset only after apply.
- **Old tests to delete**: duplicated assertions in document actions and canvas document session tests that separately verify the same post-load sequence.
- **Test environment needs**: local runtime stand-in implementing `CanvasDocumentSurface`, fake loader callbacks, fake confirmation/notification adapters. No Tauri runtime required.

## Implementation Recommendations

- The module should own all destructive document transition policy.
- It should hide sequencing details from panels, welcome screen actions, recent-file actions, template import, and runtime mount.
- It should expose status results instead of requiring callers to infer from signal state.
- It should make "dirty guard skipped" explicit for queued loads rather than relying on separate direct-load helpers.
- It should replace runtime monkey-patching with an explicit transition result or lifecycle flag owned by the document session.
- Migrate one path at a time: open-path first, then template/new/open-dialog, then queued loads, then mount-existing hydration.
close_reason: Implemented the document session transition seam, moved destructive replacement/queued-load policy behind it, added CanvasDocumentSurface.hasLoadedDocument() to replace hook monkey-patching, updated active docs, and passed focused Vitest, full Vitest, and TypeScript checks.

title: RFC: Deepen In-Canvas Map Surface Lifecycle
description:
## Problem

The in-canvas map surface has a real seam around projection and terrain helpers, but the lifecycle module remains shallow. A Preact hook still owns map construction, generation guards, camera sync, basemap status, terrain state, overlay sync, diagnostics, resize observation, and signal subscription glue.

Current friction:
- The hook is difficult to test without rendering Preact even when the behaviour is not Preact-specific.
- Async race protection is implemented through several refs that are local to the hook.
- Camera, terrain, basemap, and overlay updates share mutable lifecycle state but do not have one explicit lifecycle interface.
- Future geodetic backend work will add more cases to the same hook unless lifecycle ownership is deepened.

## Proposed Interface

Create a map surface lifecycle module that hides MapLibre ownership behind one explicit interface:

```ts
interface CanvasMapSurfaceSnapshot {
  runtime: CanvasQuerySurface | null
  location: { lat: number; lon: number } | null
  northBearingDeg: number | null
  basemapStyle: BasemapStyle
  layerVisibility: Record<string, boolean>
  layerOpacity: Record<string, number>
  terrain: TerrainLayerState
  hoveredTargets: readonly PanelTarget[]
  selectedTargets: readonly PanelTarget[]
  theme: 'light' | 'dark'
}

interface CanvasMapSurfaceLifecycle {
  attach(container: HTMLElement): void
  update(snapshot: CanvasMapSurfaceSnapshot): void
  destroy(nextState?: MapLibreCanvasSurfaceStateInput): void
}

function createCanvasMapSurfaceLifecycle(deps: CanvasMapSurfaceDeps): CanvasMapSurfaceLifecycle
```

Usage example:

```ts
const lifecycle = createCanvasMapSurfaceLifecycle({
  loadMapLibre,
  loadTerrainSupport,
  onStateChange,
})

lifecycle.attach(surfaceElement)
lifecycle.update(snapshotFromSignals())
```

The Preact adapter becomes responsible only for collecting signal snapshots and mounting/unmounting the lifecycle module.

## Dependency Strategy

**Local-substitutable**: tests provide fake MapLibre and fake terrain adapters.

Dependencies are handled by:
- A MapLibre loader adapter.
- A terrain-support loader adapter.
- A state publication callback.
- A diagnostics publication callback.
- The read-only canvas query surface.

The MapLibre instance remains a concrete adapter owned by the lifecycle module. Preact is only a mounting adapter.

## Testing Strategy

- **New seam tests to write**: attach/update/destroy lifecycle, no map without runtime/location/visible layer, exact camera update on viewport revision, style change rebuild, source-load readiness, error before ready, error after ready, terrain clear/paint/rebuild modes, generation cancellation, resize observer cleanup, overlay clear when disabled.
- **Old tests to delete**: Preact-rendered tests that exist only to exercise lifecycle state transitions and fake a full canvas runtime.
- **Test environment needs**: fake MapLibre map with event capture, fake loader promises for race tests, fake query surface, fake state callback. Preact tests should remain only for adapter mounting.

## Implementation Recommendations

- Keep projection math in the existing projection seam; do not move map math into the lifecycle module.
- Keep MapLibre non-authoritative: lifecycle update snapshots are inputs, not document mutations.
- Keep terrain diff/apply helpers separate; the lifecycle module decides when to call them.
- Preserve exact camera sync: no deadbands or skipped viewport updates.
- Migrate by first extracting an imperative lifecycle behind the current hook, then shrinking the hook to snapshot collection.
close_reason: Extracted app-level in-canvas map surface lifecycle, reduced Preact surface to snapshot/mount adapter, added direct lifecycle tests and adapter tests. Gates passed: npx tsc --noEmit; npx vitest run src/__tests__/canvas-map-surface-lifecycle.test.ts src/__tests__/maplibre-canvas-surface.test.tsx; npm test.

title: RFC: Deepen CanvasRuntime Interface
description:
## Problem

The canvas runtime module is too shallow at its current interface. `CanvasRuntime` exposes interaction commands, document lifecycle, scene queries, map projection inputs, presentation settings, species cache hydration, persistence, and teardown through one wide interface.

Current friction:
- Read-only callers such as the in-canvas map and bottom panels can see mutation and document lifecycle methods they must not use.
- Tests that only need viewport or scene reads must stub unrelated command and lifecycle methods.
- The runtime implementation still imports app-layer signal modules, which weakens locality between the canvas scene and app orchestration.
- Future map and panel work will add more query methods unless the query/projection seam gets deeper.

The deletion test shows the module is earning its keep, but its current interface leaks too much implementation shape to callers.

## Proposed Interface

Split the runtime interface into three deeper surfaces, implemented by the same runtime during migration:

```ts
interface CanvasCommandSurface {
  setTool(name: string): void
  zoomIn(): void
  zoomOut(): void
  zoomToFit(): void
  undo(): void
  redo(): void
  copy(): void
  paste(): void
  duplicateSelected(): void
  deleteSelected(): void
  selectAll(): void
  bringToFront(): void
  sendToBack(): void
  lockSelected(): void
  unlockSelected(): void
  groupSelected(): void
  ungroupSelected(): void
  toggleGrid(): void
  toggleSnapToGrid(): void
  toggleRulers(): void
}

interface CanvasQuerySurface {
  getSceneSnapshot(): ScenePersistedState
  getViewport(): SceneViewportState
  getViewportScreenSize(): { width: number; height: number }
  readonly viewportRevision: ReadonlySignal<number>
  getSelection(): Set<string>
  getPlacedPlants(): PlacedPlant[]
  getLocalizedCommonNames(): ReadonlyMap<string, string | null>
  getSelectedPlantColorContext(): SelectedPlantColorContext
  getPlantSizeMode(): PlantSizeMode
  getPlantColorByAttr(): ColorByAttribute | null
}

interface CanvasDocumentSurface {
  initializeViewport(): void
  attachRulersTo(element: HTMLElement): void
  showCanvasChrome(): void
  hideCanvasChrome(): void
  loadDocument(file: CanopiFile): void
  replaceDocument(file: CanopiFile): void
  serializeDocument(metadata: CanvasRuntimeDocumentMetadata, doc: CanopiFile): CanopiFile
  markSaved(): void
  clearHistory(): void
  resize(width: number, height: number): void
  destroy(): void
}
```

Usage example:

```ts
function syncMapSurface(runtime: CanvasQuerySurface | null) {
  if (!runtime) return
  const frame = createMapFrame(
    runtime.getViewport(),
    runtime.getViewportScreenSize(),
    location,
    northBearingDeg,
  )
}
```

The full mounted runtime can remain an intersection type during migration:

```ts
type MountedCanvasRuntime = CanvasCommandSurface & CanvasQuerySurface & CanvasDocumentSurface
```

## Dependency Strategy

**Local-substitutable**: consumers depend on the smallest local surface they need.

Dependencies are handled by:
- A command surface for toolbars and shortcuts.
- A query surface for panels and map projections.
- A document surface for document session transitions.
- Runtime construction dependencies injected from the app layer for settings, panel-target presentation, and session mirrors.

This is not a ports-and-adapters module yet because there is still one production runtime implementation. If another runtime appears, the same surfaces become real seams with multiple adapters.

## Testing Strategy

- **New seam tests to write**: panel/map tests using only `CanvasQuerySurface`, toolbar tests using only `CanvasCommandSurface`, document session tests using only `CanvasDocumentSurface`, compile-time checks preventing read-only modules from receiving command methods, runtime construction tests proving canvas/runtime modules do not import app modules directly.
- **Old tests to delete**: full-runtime mocks in map surface and panel tests; assertions that stub unrelated methods just to satisfy `CanvasRuntime`.
- **Test environment needs**: small typed stand-ins per surface and one integration test for the combined runtime implementation.

## Implementation Recommendations

- Keep one runtime implementation initially; deepen the interface before moving implementation code.
- Replace `getSceneStore()` for external callers with `getSceneSnapshot()` or a purpose-built query method so callers cannot reach mutable scene internals.
- Move app signal reads/writes behind injected runtime dependencies so canvas runtime code has locality around canvas scene behaviour.
- Migrate consumers by need: map surface and bottom panels to query surface, toolbar/shortcuts to command surface, document session to document surface.
- Only remove the old wide `CanvasRuntime` type once all consumers have moved to smaller surfaces.
close_reason: Implemented CanvasCommandSurface, CanvasQuerySurface, and CanvasDocumentSurface; migrated production callers to command/query/document accessors; replaced MapLibre mutable store access with getSceneSnapshot(); added canvas-runtime surface contract tests and query-only MapLibre test doubles. Gates: npx tsc --noEmit; focused vitest runtime/session/map/document/toolbar tests; npm test.

title: Opt-In Sensitive Problem Report Attachments
description:
## Slice Summary
Add explicit user consent controls for sensitive Problem Report attachments such as the current Design or screenshots.

## User Value
Users can provide richer evidence for hard bugs while preserving the default privacy boundary.

## Out of Scope
Automatic inclusion of Design data, background uploads, or long-term retention policy.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Add explicit consent controls for sensitive Problem Report attachments.

**Current behavior:**
The first Problem Report flow should exclude sensitive evidence by default, which means some hard-to-reproduce issues may still need a richer user-approved attachment path.

**Desired behavior:**
Users can explicitly opt in to include sensitive attachments in a Diagnostic Bundle. Each option should be clearly named, off by default, and scoped to a specific evidence type such as the current Design or a screenshot. The Report Summary or bundle manifest should identify which sensitive attachments were included.

**Key interfaces:**
- Problem Report consent controls.
- Diagnostic Bundle manifest or contents contract.
- Current Design export/read surface.
- Screenshot capture surface, if included.

**Out of scope:**
- Automatic inclusion of sensitive data.
- Remote upload or support-channel integration.
- Broad privacy policy or retention-policy work.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Sensitive attachment controls are off by default.
- [ ] Each opt-in option clearly describes what will be included.
- [ ] Included sensitive attachments are represented in the Diagnostic Bundle manifest or Report Summary.
- [ ] Declining all sensitive options preserves the v1 privacy-safe bundle behavior.
- [ ] Focused tests cover default exclusion and opt-in inclusion for each supported sensitive attachment type.

title: Follow-up: Extract canvas runtime app-signal adapters
description: SceneCanvasRuntime still imports app-level settings, panel-target, and mirror signals after canopi-dcd moved session publication to explicit runtime facades. Extract the remaining app-signal synchronization behind injected adapters without introducing a second scene owner. Keep the next slice narrow around one signal family and add focused boundary tests.
close_reason: Extracted panel-target app-signal synchronization behind an injected scene runtime adapter, mounted the app signal-backed adapter from the document session, documented the runtime boundary, and verified with focused Vitest, full frontend Vitest, and TypeScript checks.
